findings.description: Table column structure is altered. The PDF has clearly grouped columns under date headers (2/14/2014, 2/17/2014, 2/26/2014, 2/27/2014) with sub-columns for sample IDs and qualifiers. The HTML table splits these into individual columns with verbose multi-line headers, losing the visual grouping and making the qualifier columns less clearly associated with their parent date columns.
findings.evidence: PDF shows compact grouped headers like '2/14/2014 AC04986 AC04987 AC04993' as a single column group. HTML renders these as separate columns with lengthy wrapped header text like '2/14/2014 AC04986 AC04987 AC04993' and '2/17/2014 AC05085 AC05086 AC05104 Qualifier' as distinct columns.

findings.description: Some table cell alignment for 'U' qualifier values appears differently positioned relative to their data columns compared to the PDF, though the data itself is present.
findings.evidence: In PDF, 'U' qualifiers appear in dedicated narrow sub-columns right next to numeric values. In HTML, qualifier columns are full-width columns making the association less immediately clear.

findings.description: The PDF presents the entire dataset as a compact single-page table with footnotes below. The HTML renders it as a very long vertically-stretched layout with significantly more row height, fundamentally changing the compact tabular presentation into a much more spread-out format.
findings.evidence: PDF fits all data rows in a compact table on one page. HTML stretches the same content over a much longer vertical space with generous row padding.

findings.description: The footnote about Aluminum being pH and hardness dependent is rendered as a bullet list item in HTML but appears as plain text with an asterisk in the PDF.
findings.evidence: PDF: '* Aluminum is pH and hardness dependent...' as inline text. HTML: rendered as a bullet point in a list.

findings.description: The HTML table header structure is overly complex and does not clearly represent the column layout of the PDF. The PDF has distinct columns for each sampling date with sub-columns for Report #, Lab Dissolved, and Hg 1631, but the HTML merges these into confusing combined header cells with separate 'Qualifier' columns.
findings.evidence: PDF has clear column headers: '2/14/2014 AC04986 AC04987 AC04993' as one sampling event with values and qualifiers in adjacent sub-columns. HTML splits each date into two columns labeled e.g. '2/14/2014 AC04986 AC04987 AC04993' and '2/14/2014 AC04986 AC04987 AC04993 Qualifier', which is functionally acceptable but the header text 'Report # Dissolved Units\Hg 1631' is confusing compared to the PDF's separate header rows.

findings.description: The heading 'Sampling Dates and Sample ID' is presented as h3 in the HTML, but in the PDF it appears as a secondary subtitle line, not a distinct section heading. This is a minor structural choice.
findings.evidence: PDF: 'Sampling Dates and Sample ID' appears as a subtitle under the main title. HTML: rendered as <h3>.

findings.description: The footnote for Aluminum uses a bullet list format in HTML instead of the asterisk prefix used in the PDF.
findings.evidence:
PDF: '* Aluminum is pH and hardness dependent...' HTML: '
  • Aluminum is pH and hardness dependent...
'. The meaning is preserved but the formatting differs from the source's simple asterisk notation.
